Northwest Teams Strong at Intercollegiates

Eric Bone

This year saw the biggest showing yet of Washington State collegiate orienteers at the U.S. Intercollegiate Championships. The 22nd annual running of the event, hosted by Southern Michigan Orienteering Club, was held on April 9 & 10 at Yankee Springs Recreation Area near Grand Rapids, MI. Ten orienteers representing four Washington colleges, accompanied by their die-hard travel buddies, Cascade O.C. members Bruce McAlister, Paul Magnus and Peter Golde, enjoyed the great weather, beautiful open woods and interesting courses at this smoothly-run event.

The large turnout was facilitated greatly by the travel support generously contributed by Bruce, Peter, and WSOA. The gang flew in on the red-eye Friday morning, then stopped for breakfast on the way to their heated cabin, which was conveniently located on the competition area. Participants enjoyed nearby starts and finishes on both days, as well as a delicious pasta dinner on Saturday. (The alfredo sauce was heavenly!)

A few special happenings punctuated the trip for the Washington crew. Gabe Wingard from Pacific Lutheran University had his 23rd birthday—complete with cake, singing and good cheer—on Saturday after the dinner. Also, Paul Magnus (apparently happy about seeing his brother John, who came to stay with the group in the cabin) had a phenomenal first day run in the men's open competition (M-21-A) on the Blue course. Paul placed third on Saturday among a strong field, coming in just seconds behind U.S. Team member Tom Bruce and beating Canadian Mark Adams, one of the top orienteers in North America. Way to go, Paul!

As team captain Bill Cusworth prophesied after last year's nationals, the U.W. Huskies continued to build their dynasty, again fending off a challenge from long-time orienteering powerhouse West Point. The Huskies' winning effort was spearheaded by graduate student Rune Hoel, who posted the fastest overall time in the two day competition. Joining him in a purple and gold sweep of the top two places was returning Individual Men's Champion Eric Bone, who was awarded the title again since Rune, a Norwegian citizen, is not eligible for individual honors. Also turning in solid performances for the Husky team were returning graduate student and captain Bill Cusworth and John Whitmer, a talented newcomer who blazed his way to an amazing fifth place finish on day two—only his third day of orienteering competition ever!

Aside from the U.W. team, the Washington squad was graced by some familiar faces. This year two seasoned school league alumni came for their first time to challenge the collegiate women's field. True to form, both Lisa Nordlund representing Western Washington University and Ellen Boomer of the University of Puget Sound garnered medals

in the individual competition. Rumor has it that Lisa has signed with the UW and will be helping keep the winning tradition alive next year.

Another former WIOL competitor was Gabe Wingard, whose PLU team achieved a fourth place finish in their national debut. Other PLU athletes were Norwegians Harald Gunderson, who lead the team by placing well on both days, Kjell Thompsen, and Arilg Jacobsen.

Although the PLU team was bested by three teams from the formidable field of orienteers, they left no doubt that nobody outparties them. Taking full advantage of the three-hour time zone change, they went out late Saturday evening to check out the night life at a tavern in the nearby town of Hasting ("wasting" as some locals say). Gabe, apparently exhausted by the effort of growing a whole year older, had retired to his bunk to digest his birthday cake and the pasta from dinner. Rather than calling it quits when the tavern closed at 2 a.m., the Norwegian trio—who had quickly become the center of attention—went to a private party they had been invited to. The devoted party animals finally crashed on their bunks not long before everyone else got up.

All-in-all, everybody had a great time in Michigan. Tune in next year when the Intercollegiates are being hosted by Land of the Vikings Orienteering Club.

The Charlie Challenge #3

by Rick Hood, Meet Director

For the third year in a row, COC is hosting a 6 and 3 hour Rogaine at Drunken Charlie Lake this May 14th. Oh sure, you've heard a few rumors about the area like, "it's not for the faint of heart or foot" but ask around and most will also say, "it is an event not to be missed as it is some of the best challenging fun around".

If the term Rogaine is new to you, think of it as an extended team navigation score orienteering event. Basically, you are given some planning time prior to the mass start as you team determines which of the 20 or so controls to go to and in what order. Each of the controls has an announced point value and the team that come back on time with the most points wins. (Just like in chess, the rules are easy, now as to strategy...)

Since last year's event was so successful (judging from your comments) we elected not to modify our approach too much. The mass start for the six hour event is again at 11am at the lake, with the finish in the same place by 5pm. Any team can come back early or for a break, but those on the competitive edge will take advantage of the full 6 hours. (Like last year pre-marked maps will be available at 10:15 for those teams that pre-registered with Rick at 367-2606 by Thursday the 12th of May.)

For those looking either a little less of or a more compressed challenge, try the 3 hour option. Your team may start at either 11:30, 12:00, 12:30, or 1:00. (Again, if you pre-register, you get a pre-marked map and are assured of food at the finish.)

Of course we will again have Great Harvest Bread with honey and PB, fruit, and drink available throughout the day, with our BBQ starting around 4:00. Like last year, the fee is $10 per person, which includes your map and food. We are limiting the event to 50 teams, where at least one team member must be an experienced orienteer over 18 years of age; 40 team slots are reserved for pre-registered teams and 10 team spaces for day-of-event registration, but the latter will have to mark their maps (please allow 45 minutes for this and if you are running the six hour event, this will eat up most of your planning time.) Note, for safety reasons, we require every participant to be a part of a team; if you need a partner(s) please give Rick a call, we'd love to match you up and have you try the Challenge of Charlie this year.

As to the course, look for a little different flavor from the new DCLR design team of Bill Cusworth and Paul Magnus. (Both Bill and Paul are partial, as the rumor goes, to chocolate chip cookies, so if you are thinking of a bribe... Actually, these guys are above reproach but they do like cookies. Moreover, their course is rumored to be fun and fast, but you be the judge.)

We will have the road signs starting at the town of Duvall at the corner of the Woodinville-Duvall and State #203, just like last year. Please allow 75 minutes from Seattle to reach the event center at the Lake as well. Lastly we will have two water controls and water at the event center, but you may want to carry your own water as well.

Night Navigation Training

On Friday, May 13th (the night before Drunken Charlie), the Sammamish O' Club is running a night navigation training event. The Night Nav event will be at Farrel-McWhirter Park in Redmond, WA, starting at 8 PM, finishing by 11 PM. Pre-registration is required, so we can plan pre-

Page 3

marked maps and figure the entry fee to cover park costs—about $5.00 per person. Call (206)487-0099 to pre-register, and call the O' Hotline for any schedule changes at (206)783-FUNO.

The parking lot at Farrel-McWhirter will be locked up after about 9 PM, and reopened when the event finishes. Try to arrive between 8 and 8:30 PM, or come early if you wish. We'll offer informal training in how to move around and not get lost at night. There will be a score course laid out around the meadow area and in the forest. Most controls will be quite easy, and there is almost no chance of getting lost in this small Redmond park. One or two hard controls will be set for advanced navigators, based on ideas received from various people.

You will need to bring a good compass, a headlamp if you have one, or flashlight(s). This event is set up for teams who will be doing 12 or 24-hour rogaines in the future, so bring your partner, or we will match you with someone. Please carry the mandatory whistle.

If you're visiting from out of town, check with locals or leave a message on the Hotline (783-FUNO) for guest quarters somewhere. To get to Farrel-McWhirter Park, go east on 520 through downtown Redmond to Avondale Road, then north to turn right on Novelty Hill Road, east to Redmond road and follow park signs.

Bellingham Area Orienteers

by Lisa Nordland

We of the Bellingham area have decided that we are tired of driving down to Seattle for most of the meets, so we formed our own club: The Chuckanut Orienteers, or CHUKO.
Our first meet was held in Cornwall Park in April. This generated some interest from Whatcom County people who had never seen orienteering before. CHUKO is now 7 members strong and growing. We are working on mapping the Western Washington University campus, where we hope to have a meet soon, and we are investigating other potential sites.
CHUKO will have a club meeting on May 2, at 7pm. We'll be in room 461 in the Viking Union Building at WWU.
Right now we still have more T shirts than members, but a high percentage of our members are new or newly active orienteers. We expect to keep growing quickly as more people in the area find out about us.
If you know someone in the Bellingham area who is interested, let them know about our meeting or have them call us at 734-6712.

Cle Elum Ridge Big Event

As most of you know, this Sept. 10/11, COC will host two great days of orienteering on the fast and fun Cle Elum Ridge terrain. We can use all the help we can get in hosting this event and save for the key meet staff, we won't ask anyone to pass up competing on the fine courses set by Peter Golde. Especially on Saturday, when we will host the first-ever US Orienteering Federation Short Course Championships, where each participant will run two short courses with a few hours rest between, we really want to do a super job for our out-of-state visitors. But given our "quality yet simple" approach, we think the event will be a fun time, even for our meet staff; especially if we can get a lot of you to pitch in.

On Sunday, there will be traditional one-day A meet offerings, from white to blue course, that will also include a super blue course of 14km for those male elite orienteers. (The women elite will run the regular blue at 10.5km.)

We have booked the entire camp Koinonia for camping/bunkspace and dinning with a special package for meet staff. So whether you are coming just to compete or are willing to help out as well, we thing we can promise you a good time in the woods (OK, open woods with nice fields) with some super folks this Sept. 10/11th.

Our complete flyer, with all the details will be available in June. If you want more information though, please just give me a call at 367-2606.

Submission Guidelines
Bearing 315 welcomes submissions of articles, letters, or photographs about orienteering. If you would like your submission returned, please include a self-addressed envelope. The submission deadline is the 23rd of each even month for the issue to be mailed on the following 1st.
Submissions should be mailed to:
Bearing 315
c/o Peter Golde
14422 NE 42nd Pl. #801
Bellevue, WA 98007
If possible, please submit on an IBM or Macintosh diskette, or submit via electronic mail to petergo@microsoft.com. If you use a word processor, please submit in Microsoft Word (Mac, DOS, or Windows), WordPerfect, or RTF format; plain text format is also an option. Meet directors, please send meet results to Debbie Newell for compilation.

Mapper Sought

We are doing a lot of the early work to get ready for COC's "A" meet this fall at Cle Elum. One of the important tasks is to bring the map up to date with the new trails, logging roads, vegetation changes, etc. By May 15th we would like to find out if anyone in the local area who has mapping experience would be interested in doing the work. We would like to give preference to COC members and local area mappers before we have to reach out farther. The timing is tight so we hope someone local will jump on this.

We've been out to the area recently and expect that the field checking effort would involve one weekend of work (approximately 15 hours) and probably 5 hours of drafting at home. The club will compensate the mapper here especially given the importance of the event that this work is targeted for.

Please give Mike Fritz a call (481-5877) if you are interested and have some idea in mind about what type of compensation you would think reasonable for this. Also consider that this work is going to have to be done in a timely fashion with high quality. You should plan on doing the field checking in May and early June with a deadline of June 15th. The drafting needs to be finished by August 5th.

Two Day Events Fill Summer

As usual, as the summer weather starts to roll around, the orienteering focus begins to shift from our urban parks to multi-day events in the real outdoors. This summer is no exception, and a host of great two (and more) day events fill most of the summer, especially May, June, and September. If you are interested in going to any of these events, I suggest you call either the meet organizers or someone from the local area to get information on lodging, carpooling, and so forth. It both increases the fun and decreases the expense to drive and stay with other people. Here are the main highlights of the summer season:
The 6-hour Drunken Charlie Lake Rogaine kicks off the summer season this year on May 14. Although not strictly a two-day, there is a night-O training event the evening before and a following Canoe-O; it should be quite a weekend. Besides, six hours at Drunken Charlie is more tiring than most two-day events. May 21-22 is the annual Sage Stomp event in Kamloops. If you've never been to a Kamloops event, you really should try one—they're loads of fun and the orienteering is always excellent. The cost is CAN$10/day for USOF members and is well worth it; there is a BBQ at Knutsford on Saturday for CAN$7. The deadline for pre-marked maps is May 16, so call Murry in BC at 604 374-6497 ASAP.
Newcastle Island hosts a two-day on the May 28-29; I don't know too much about this one. June 11/12 is the ever popular Aspen Grove area between Hope and Merritt. This map is literally in the middle of nowhere, so you'll want to bring your tent and some food along to stay at the primitive (pit toilets only) camping right next to the meet site. The nearest lodging and food is in Merritt, about 30 miles away. The map here is really nice.
June 20-23 is the U.S. Championships in Anchorage. This is a three-day event; an individual "A" on Monday, then the Championships proper on Wednesday and Thursday. June 25-26 is the 24 hour North American Rogaine Championships near Kamloops. The ultimate test! I've heard the terrain there is ideal for Rogaining. The deadline is June 18th if you want to save $5 ($35/person).
June 27-July 3 is the Colorado five-day, which actually features six days of orienteering in the high-country of Colorado on some excellent maps there, if you can make the drive. In the other direction the Blue Lake training camp in Alberta is also this weekend on July 1-3. On July 30-31 Sammamish hosts what should be an excellent two-day at Ros-Elum ridge near Cle Elum. Ros-Elum is always both a physical and technical challenge (many call it the overall hardest O map they've ever run on), and the Sammamish crew will make it fair and fun, I'm sure.
* August appears to be a fairly quiet month around the Northwest (does someone want to course-set an August event? We would love to have some more). Some of us will go back east for the six-day Canadian champs/North American champs/USOF convention in the Rochester/Ontario area.

Page 5

The pace picks up again in September: the 3rd and 4th are the dates for the BC O' Champs at Prince George. Following that up on Sept. 10-11 is COC's Cle Elum Ridge "A" meet, featuring the U.S. Short Course Championship. Rick's article in this issue previews this one.
* Finally, the summer season officially closes with the always enjoyable Inland Empire Cup at Riverside State Park in Spokane, October 1-2. This map boasts some of the fastest and most open forest ever seen in orienteering, but doesn't sacrifice the technical challenge either. And the Bowl & Pitcher campground across the river is a fantastic place to stay.

Training Events

by Eric Bone

Greetings, fellow orienteering fanatics. As the Cascade O.C. training coordinator, I'm happy to tell you that a few orienteering training sessions are being tentatively planned to fill the voids in the summer schedule. These will probably involve some low-key, fun exercises on a couple of weekends or Wednesday evenings when there are no other events going on. More information on this will be posted at upcoming meets and on the orienteering hot-line (783-FUNO), so stay tuned. Call Eric at (206) 634-2279 with any questions or comments. See you in the forest!

Bird-a-thon

Marti Anderson is participating in a "bird-a-thon" fundraiser for the Seattle Audubon Society. SAS, founded in 1916, in one of the largest and oldest environmental groups in the Pacific Northwest. It's goal is preservation of our natural history through study, education, and sharing enjoyment with others. Its focus is both local and international.
Marti will be doing her bird-a-thon at everyone's favorite O venues, Marymoor State Park and Bridle Trails State Park (well her favorites, anyway). If you would like more information on how you can lend your support, please call or write Marti at 284-4549; 2323 W. Boston #8, Seattle 98199.

Field Notes

by Rick Hood

* On Sunday the 5th of June, we would love to have your help with our trail project; we will be building a 20 foot bridge that spans a creek bed. We hope to finish the bridge by mid-afternoon and your help would really make a difference! Not only with this bridge effort open up the new South Canyon trail that we build last year, is a great way to demonstrate how interested the local orienteering commu-

Page 6

nity is in preserving and enhancing our rapport with the St. Edward Park (the site of COC's annual Bog Slog) specifically and all public land in general. In brief, it confirms we are the good guys. So please, give a day of your time for this worth cause. We will meet in the swimming pool parking lot at 8:30 Sunday morning on the 5th of June and plan to wrap things up at 3:30; please bring a lunch and wear work clothes. Call at 367-2606 if you are planning to come.
On Saturday the 4th of June, more folks will be hearing about and trying orienteering. The Washington Trails Association has organized an outdoor fair in North Bend at the elementary school. Hiking, climbing, conservation, canoeing, x-c skiing clubs, and others plus both media and political representatives will converge to celebrate National Trails Day. Mike Schuh is preparing a small map of the area and Rick Hood and Dave Lilly will be hosting a booth and running a micro-O event. If you would like to help out or want to check out some of the other fine offerings, give me a call. Saying "new maps are underway" is music to an orienteers ears. Sammamish is gearing up for the Bull Frog flats area between Ros Elum Ridge and the freeway and Shoreline Community College. Cascade's new OCAD Fire Mountain map will get an update and the San Juan English/American Camp project has crossed another hurdle. Got some mapping ideas, give SAMM's Dave Enger or COC's Debbie Newell a call, as mapping coordinators, they always are on the lookout for worth projects. Please note, that once an area is announced as a future map site, running/training on said area is generally considered bad form until the first meet is held there. Thanks for your understand and cooperation.
Linda and I got a nice card from Peter Bonek and Claudia Kunzel. They are settling in Vienna; Peter has started work for Phillips and Claudia is finishing her studies as well as traveling with the national team. If anyone is traveling that way, give us a call and we will pass on their address. And best of luck to the some 20 orienteers that are flying to Alaska this June for the US Orienteering Championships! Debbie Newell, Eric Bone, Fred Veler, and others are psyched for this event. Please join me in wishing them much success and a whale of a good time.
* Our Debbie Newell, not only a member of the US Orienteering Federation national team, is now also their "Team News" editor. Power to the press and the Northwest. (By the way, the rumor about her sneaking in a COC and SAMM membership form in each issue of the "Team News" is not true, nor is the one about her an Mel Gibson teaming up for the Drunken Charlie Lake Rogaine this May 14th.)

Upcoming Meetings

Sammamish O.C. will be having a club meeting on Tue, May 10 at Bob Reddick's house at 7:30pm. All members are invited. Call Bob at 487-0099 for directions.
COC will host a BBQ potluck and short O-meet in Finn Hill Park on Wednesday, June 8, starts begin at 5:30. The event will be at Bruce McAlister's, 14014 81st Pl. NE, Bothell. His home is adjacent to Fill Hill Park north of the ball fields, and is reached off of NE 141st (821-6862 if you get lost. A COC board meeting will follow at 7:30. Bruce and Bill Cusworth have updated the sketch map some of you may be familiar with from past years, and will have their own map of the part of Finn Hill Park east of Juanita Drive (we keep hearing rumors that Nathan Tharp has drawn a complete Finn Hill OCAD map). This will be your last chance to see "old" Finn Hill Park, as Fill Hill Park is currently under construction). Starts will be 5:30 to 7:00 pm in Bruce's driveway; the potluck barbecue starts at 6. A gas grill will be available, and beverages will be provided.
Those who wish may attend the board meeting, or just hang out at the barbecue.

Airline Discount Available

For 1994 USOF has arranged a 5-10% discount with United when travelling to "A" meets including the convention. You need not be a USOF member. Call the Meeting Reservations Center 1-800-521-4041 & reference ID Number 541YG. There is also a discount on Hertz cars when using this service.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?
The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?
The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local ("C") events with color maps. "A" and "B" meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.

Beyond Orienteering

Wanda Howlett (SAMM) recently won a bicycle time trial race in Ravensdale. She completed the 4-mile course in 10:08, beating not only everyone in her category, but also everyone (including the men) in the next-highest category. Since learning in January that, because of bone problems she can't run any more, Wanda has focused her energies on bicycling, and her ultimate goal is to compete at U.S. Nationals.
In March, she and husband Mark (SAMM) won the coed team division at the Vance Creek Duathlon (3-mile run, 13.5-mile bike, 2-mile run), where Wanda had the fastest women's bicycle leg (and was in the top 3 overall). Debbie Newell (COC) and Dave Tallent (SAMM) competed as solo participants in the Vance Creek race.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

block_index.text: BEARING 315 The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest May, 1994 (Cascade Orienteering Logo)

This year saw the biggest showing yet of Washington State collegiate orienteers at the U.S. Intercollegiate Championships. The 22nd annual running of the event, hosted by Southern Michigan Orienteering Club, was held on April 9 & 10 at Yankee Springs Recreation Area near Grand Rapids, MI. Ten orienteers representing four Washington colleges, accompanied by their die-hard travel buddies, Cascade O.C. members Bruce McAlister, Paul Magnus and Peter Golde, enjoyed the great weather, beautiful open woods and interesting courses at this smoothly-run event.

The large turnout was facilitated greatly by the travel support generously contributed by Bruce, Peter, and WSOA. The gang flew in on the red-eye Friday morning, then stopped for breakfast on the way to their heated cabin, which was conveniently located on the competition area. Participants enjoyed nearby starts and finishes on both days, as well as a delicious pasta dinner on Saturday. (The alfredo sauce was heavenly!)

A few special happenings punctuated the trip for the Washington crew. Gabe Wingard from Pacific Lutheran University had his 23rd birthday—complete with cake, singing and good cheer—on Saturday after the dinner. Also, Paul Magnus (apparently happy about seeing his brother John, who came to stay with the group in the cabin) had a phenomenal first day run in the men's open competition (M-21-A) on the Blue course. Paul placed third on Saturday among a strong field, coming in just seconds behind U.S. Team member Tom Bruce and beating Canadian Mark Adams, one of the top orienteers in North America. Way to go, Paul!

As team captain Bill Cusworth prophesied after last year's nationals, the U.W. Huskies continued to build their dynasty, again fending off a challenge from long-time orienteering powerhouse West Point. The Huskies' winning effort was spearheaded by graduate student Rune Hoel, who posted the fastest overall time in the two day competition. Joining him in a purple and gold sweep of the top two places was returning Individual Men's Champion Eric Bone, who was awarded the title again since Rune, a Norwegian citizen, is not eligible for individual honors. Also turning in solid performances for the Husky team were returning graduate student and captain Bill Cusworth and John Whitmer, a talented newcomer who blazed his way to an amazing fifth place finish on day two—only his third day of orienteering competition ever!

Aside from the U.W. team, the Washington squad was graced by some familiar faces. This year two seasoned school league alumni came for their first time to challenge the collegiate women's field. True to form, both Lisa Nordlund representing Western Washington University and Ellen Boomer of the University of Puget Sound garnered medals

Copyright © 1994, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for free distribution for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club.
in the individual competition. Rumor has it that Lisa has signed with the UW and will be helping keep the winning tradition alive next year.

Another former WIOL competitor was Gabe Wingard, whose PLU team achieved a fourth place finish in their national debut. Other PLU athletes were Norwegians Harald Gunderson, who lead the team by placing well on both days, Kjell Thompsen, and Arilg Jacobsen.

Although the PLU team was bested by three teams from the formidable field of orienteers, they left no doubt that nobody outparties them. Taking full advantage of the three-hour time zone change, they went out late Saturday evening to check out the night life at a tavern in the nearby town of Hasting ("wasting" as some locals say). Gabe, apparently exhausted by the effort of growing a whole year older, had retired to his bunk to digest his birthday cake and the pasta from dinner. Rather than calling it quits when the tavern closed at 2 a.m., the Norwegian trio—who had quickly become the center of attention—went to a private party they had been invited to. The devoted party animals finally crashed on their bunks not long before everyone else got up.
All-in-all, everybody had a great time in Michigan. Tune in next year when the Intercollegiates are being hosted by Land of the Vikings Orienteering Club.

For the third year in a row, COC is hosting a 6 and 3 hour Rogaine at Drunken Charlie Lake this May 14th. Oh sure, you've heard a few rumors about the area like, "it's not for the faint of heart or foot" but ask around and most will also say, "it is an event not to be missed as it is some of the best challenging fun around".

If the term Rogaine is new to you, think of it as an extended team navigation score orienteering event. Basically, you are given some planning time prior to the mass start as you team determines which of the 20 or so controls to go to and in what order. Each of the controls has an announced point value and the team that come back on time with the most points wins. (Just like in chess, the rules are easy, now as to strategy...)

Since last year's event was so successful (judging from your comments) we elected not to modify our approach too much. The mass start for the six hour event is again at 11am at the lake, with the finish in the same place by 5pm. Any team can come back early or for a break, but those on the competitive edge will take advantage of the full 6 hours. (Like last year pre-marked maps will be available at 10:15 for those teams that pre-registered with Rick at 367-2606 by Thursday the 12th of May.)

For those looking either a little less of or a more compressed challenge, try the 3 hour option. Your team may start at either 11:30, 12:00, 12:30, or 1:00. (Again, if you pre-register, you get a pre-marked map and are assured of food at the finish.)

Of course we will again have Great Harvest Bread with honey and PB, fruit, and drink available throughout the day, with our BBQ starting around 4:00. Like last year, the fee is $10 per person, which includes your map and food. We are limiting the event to 50 teams, where at least one team member must be an experienced orienteer over 18 years of age; 40 team slots are reserved for pre-registered teams and 10 team spaces for day-of-event registration, but the latter will have to mark their maps (please allow 45 minutes for this and if you are running the six hour event, this will eat up most of your planning time.) Note, for safety reasons, we require every participant to be a part of a team; if you need a partner(s) please give Rick a call, we'd love to match you up and have you try the Challenge of Charlie this year.

As to the course, look for a little different flavor from the new DCLR design team of Bill Cusworth and Paul Magnus. (Both Bill and Paul are partial, as the rumor goes, to chocolate chip cookies, so if you are thinking of a bribe... Actually, these guys are above reproach but they do like cookies. Moreover, their course is rumored to be fun and fast, but you be the judge.)

We will have the road signs starting at the town of Duvall at the corner of the Woodinville-Duvall and State #203, just like last year. Please allow 75 minutes from Seattle to reach the event center at the Lake as well. Lastly we will have two water controls and water at the event center, but you may want to carry your own water as well.

On Friday, May 13th (the night before Drunken Charlie), the Sammamish O' Club is running a night navigation training event. The Night Nav event will be at Farrel-McWhirter Park in Redmond, WA, starting at 8 PM, finishing by 11 PM. Pre-registration is required, so we can plan pre-
marked maps and figure the entry fee to cover park costs—about $5.00 per person. Call (206)487-0099 to pre-register, and call the O' Hotline for any schedule changes at (206)783-FUNO.

The parking lot at Farrel-McWhirter will be locked up after about 9 PM, and reopened when the event finishes. Try to arrive between 8 and 8:30 PM, or come early if you wish. We'll offer informal training in how to move around and not get lost at night. There will be a score course laid out around the meadow area and in the forest. Most controls will be quite easy, and there is almost no chance of getting lost in this small Redmond park. One or two hard controls will be set for advanced navigators, based on ideas received from various people.

You will need to bring a good compass, a headlamp if you have one, or flashlight(s). This event is set up for teams who will be doing 12 or 24-hour rogaines in the future, so bring your partner, or we will match you with someone. Please carry the mandatory whistle.

If you're visiting from out of town, check with locals or leave a message on the Hotline (783-FUNO) for guest quarters somewhere. To get to Farrel-McWhirter Park, go east on 520 through downtown Redmond to Avondale Road, then north to turn right on Novelty Hill Road, east to Redmond road and follow park signs.

We of the Bellingham area have decided that we are tired of driving down to Seattle for most of the meets, so we formed our own club: The Chuckanut Orienteers, or CHUKO. Our first meet was held in Cornwall Park in April. This generated some interest from Whatcom County people who had never seen orienteering before. CHUKO is now 7 members strong and growing. We are working on mapping the Western Washington University campus, where we hope to have a meet soon, and we are investigating other potential sites. CHUKO will have a club meeting on May 2, at 7pm. We'll be in room 461 in the Viking Union Building at WWU. Right now we still have more T shirts than members, but a high percentage of our members are new or newly active orienteers. We expect to keep growing quickly as more people in the area find out about us. If you know someone in the Bellingham area who is interested, let them know about our meeting or have them call us at 734-6712.

As most of you know, this Sept. 10/11, COC will host two great days of orienteering on the fast and fun Cle Elum Ridge terrain. We can use all the help we can get in hosting this event and save for the key meet staff, we won't ask anyone to pass up competing on the fine courses set by Peter Golde. Especially on Saturday, when we will host the first-ever US Orienteering Federation Short Course Championships, where each participant will run two short courses with a few hours rest between, we really want to do a super job for our out-of-state visitors. But given our "quality yet simple" approach, we think the event will be a fun time, even for our meet staff; especially if we can get a lot of you to pitch in.

On Sunday, there will be traditional one-day A meet offerings, from white to blue course, that will also include a super blue course of 14km for those male elite orienteers. (The women elite will run the regular blue at 10.5km.)

We have booked the entire camp Koinonia for camping/bunkspace and dinning with a special package for meet staff. So whether you are coming just to compete or are willing to help out as well, we thing we can promise you a good time in the woods (OK, open woods with nice fields) with some super folks this Sept. 10/11th.
Our complete flyer, with all the details will be available in June. If you want more information though, please just give me a call at 367-2606.

We are doing a lot of the early work to get ready for COC's "A" meet this fall at Cle Elum. We would like to give preference to COC members and local area mappers before we have to reach out farther. The timing is tight so we hope someone local will jump on this.

We've been out to the area recently and expect that the field checking effort would involve one weekend of work (approximately 15 hours) and probably 5 hours of drafting at home. The club will compensate the mapper here especially given the importance of the event that this work is targeted for.

As usual, as the summer weather starts to roll around, the orienteering focus begins to shift from our urban parks to multi-day events in the real outdoors. This summer is no exception, and a host of great two (and more) day events fill most of the summer, especially May, June, and September. If you are interested in going to any of these events, I suggest you call either the meet organizers or someone from the local area to get information on lodging, carpooling, and so forth. It both increases the fun and decreases the expense to drive and stay with other people. Here are the main highlights of the summer season: The 6-hour Drunken Charlie Lake Rogaine kicks off the summer season this year on May 14. Although not strictly a two-day, there is a night-O training event the evening before and a following Canoe-O; it should be quite a weekend. Besides, six hours at Drunken Charlie is more tiring than most two-day events. May 21-22 is the annual Sage Stomp event in Kamloops. If you've never been to a Kamloops event, you really should try one—they're loads of fun and the orienteering is always excellent. The cost is CAN$10/day for USOF members and is well worth it; there is a BBQ at Knutsford on Saturday for CAN$7. The deadline for pre-marked maps is May 16, so call Murry in BC at 604 374-6497 ASAP. Newcastle Island hosts a two-day on the May 28-29; I don't know too much about this one. June 11/12 is the ever popular Aspen Grove area between Hope and Merritt. This map is literally in the middle of nowhere, so you'll want to bring your tent and some food along to stay at the primitive (pit toilets only) camping right next to the meet site. The nearest lodging and food is in Merritt, about 30 miles away. The map here is really nice. June 20-23 is the U.S. Championships in Anchorage. This is a three-day event; an individual "A" on Monday, then the Championships proper on Wednesday and Thursday. June 25-26 is the 24 hour North American Rogaine Championships near Kamloops. The ultimate test! I've heard the terrain there is ideal for Rogaining. The deadline is June 18th if you want to save $5 ($35/person). June 27-July 3 is the Colorado five-day, which actually features six days of orienteering in the high-country of Colorado on some excellent maps there, if you can make the drive. In the other direction the Blue Lake training camp in Alberta is also this weekend on July 1-3. On July 30-31 Sammamish hosts what should be an excellent two-day at Ros-Elum ridge near Cle Elum. Ros-Elum is always both a physical and technical challenge (many call it the overall hardest O map they've ever run on), and the Sammamish crew will make it fair and fun, I'm sure. August appears to be a fairly quiet month around the Northwest (does someone want to course-set an August event? We would love to have some more). Some of us will go back east for the six-day Canadian champs/North American champs/USOF convention in the Rochester/Ontario area.

The pace picks up again in September: the 3rd and 4th are the dates for the BC O' Champs at Prince George. Following that up on Sept. 10-11 is COC's Cle Elum Ridge "A" meet, featuring the U.S. Short Course Championship. Rick's article in this issue previews this one. Finally, the summer season officially closes with the always enjoyable Inland Empire Cup at Riverside State Park in Spokane, October 1-2. This map boasts some of the fastest and most open forest ever seen in orienteering, but doesn't sacrifice the technical challenge either. And the Bowl & Pitcher campground across the river is a fantastic place to stay.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs? The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need? The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
